facts.text: Georg Ebers was born on March 1, 1837, in Berlin.
Ebers was a posthumous child, born a fortnight after his father's death on February 14, 1837.
Ebers's childhood home was at No. 4 Thiergartenstrasse in Berlin.
Ebers's childhood home included a field, meadow, orchard, dense shrubbery, a hill, and a pond.
Ebers's mother was thirty-five years old at the time of his birth.
Ebers had four siblings, including one little brother who died in infancy.
Ebers's mother chose their residence because she envisioned it as providing health and freedom for her children.
Ebers's mother told him that posthumous children were always Fortune's favorites.
Ebers's father was one of the Berlin gentlemen whose spirit of self-sacrifice and taste for art contributed to the Konigstadt Theater's prosperity.
Ebers's father was acquainted with Carl von Holtei, a dramatist and actor.
Ebers's mother was born in Holland.
Ebers's father met his mother in Holland when he was a young man.
Ebers's father's family initially did not take seriously his intention to marry a foreigner.
Ebers's father was the eldest son of one of the richest families in Berlin.
Ebers's father was nineteen years old when he decided to marry Ebers's mother.
Ebers's mother's father initially refused to listen to Ebers's father's courtship.
Ebers's father was sent to Bordeaux to acquire commercial experience.
Ebers's mother was described as beautiful, with soft blue eyes and feminine grace.
Ebers received his early education at Froebel's institute, Keilhau.
Ebers studied at the Quedlinburg Gymnasium.
Ebers attended the University of Gottingen.
Ebers experienced health problems that forced him to lead a quieter life during his student years.
Ebers's health problems led him to begin his Egyptian studies.
Ebers wrote his first novel, An Egyptian Princess, which was finished in 1863.
Ebers traveled to Egypt and discovered the Ebers Papyrus, a treatise on medicine dating from the second century B.C.
Ebers wrote a treatise on Egypt and the Book of Moses.
Ebers received a professorship at the University of Gottingen in 1864.
Ebers married the daughter of a burgomaster of Riga soon after 1864.
Ebers's wife was his active helpmate in managing business details of his works and their American and English editions.
Ebers was called to the University of Leipzig to fill the chair of Egyptology.
Ebers went to Egypt again in 1872.
Ebers unearthed the Ebers Papyrus during excavations at Thebes.
Ebers published Uarda in 1876, twelve years after An Egyptian Princess.
Ebers wrote novels including Homo Sum, The Sisters, The Emperor, and others.
Ebers began his series of tales of the middle ages and the dawn of the modern era in 1881 with The Burgomaster's Wife.
Ebers's health forced him to resign his chair at the University of Leipzig in 1889.
Ebers continued his intellectual activity despite his health problems until his death.
Ebers's last novel was Arachne.
Ebers died on August 7, 1898, at the Villa Ebers in Tutzing on the Starenberg Lake near Munich.
Ebers wrote sixteen novels.
Ebers wrote many treatises on Egyptology.
Ebers wrote two great works of reference on Egypt and Palestine.
Ebers wrote short stories, fairy tales, and biographies.
Ebers was born under what he considered a lucky star.
Ebers had restless student days at Gottingen.
Ebers was enthusiastic about Froebel's educational method.
Ebers considered himself a good German in the largest sense of the word.
Ebers lived through the turbulent days of 1848 in Berlin.
Ebers demonstrated fortitude in adversity and suffered patiently.
Ebers was greatly gifted by Nature.
Ebers dedicated his autobiography to his three sons.
Ebers's oldest son pursued the healing arts.
Ebers's second son answered his country's call.
Ebers's third son was still working to form his mind.
Ebers valued love for mankind, love for native land, and blessed toil.
Ebers wrote his autobiography from childhood to 1863, when An Egyptian Princess was finished.
Ebers's autobiography was written with literary charm and deftness of character drawing.
Ebers was influenced by the brothers Grimm.
Ebers was influenced by Friedrich Froebel.
Ebers received the foundation of his education at Froebel's institute, Keilhau.
Ebers discussed Froebel's method and Pestalozzi's method with enthusiasm and understanding.
Ebers's mother hugged her fatherless child and called him her 'comfort child.'
Georg Ebers' father was the eldest son of one of the richest families in Berlin.
Georg Ebers' father was nineteen years old when he wished to marry a foreigner.
Georg Ebers' maternal grandfather initially refused to listen to the young suitor.
Georg Ebers' paternal grandmother traveled by carriage drawn by four horses to meet his mother.
Georg Ebers' mother's rare beauty and grace won the heart of his paternal grandmother.
Georg Ebers' parents were required to wait a few years to test the sincerity of their affection.
Georg Ebers' father was sent to Bordeaux to acquire the ability to manage his father's banking business.
Georg Ebers' mother caught smallpox and feared her face would be disfigured.
Georg Ebers' father did not hesitate to marry his fiancée despite her smallpox, valuing her purity and goodness.
Georg Ebers' mother did not bear any scars from her smallpox illness.
The burgomaster of Rotterdam told Georg Ebers' father that he gave him the pearl of Rotterdam.
Georg Ebers' father died while his mother was traveling to Berlin as a bride.
Georg Ebers' mother had only slight mastery of German when she arrived in Berlin.
Georg Ebers' mother had no friends or relatives of her own family in Berlin.
Georg Ebers' mother became known as the 'beautiful Hollander' in Berlin society.
Baron von Humboldt, Rauch, and Schleiermacher escorted Georg Ebers' mother to dinner.
Hegel won a blackened coin from Georg Ebers' mother at whist and kept it.
Georg Ebers' father died just before Georg Ebers' birth.
The great wealth of Georg Ebers' family was much diminished after his father's death.
Georg Ebers' mother resolved that her sons should make their way by their own industry.
Georg Ebers' mother's brothers became able officials in the Dutch colonial service.
Moritz von Oppenfeld was Georg Ebers' father's cousin and married an Ebers.
Moritz von Oppenfeld lived in a house on the Pariser Platz, now occupied by the French embassy.
Georg Ebers' father died in Leipzigerstrasse.
Georg Ebers was born two weeks after his father's death.
Georg Ebers was reported to be an unusually sturdy and merry child.
Frau Mosson reported that Georg Ebers laughed on the third day of his life.
Georg Ebers' mother moved the family to No. 4 Thiergartenstrasse to obtain pure air and room for exercise.
Frau Kommissionsrath Reichert, the owner of the Thiergartenstrasse house, had also lost her husband.
Frau Reichert was initially reluctant to rent to a large family of children.
Frau Reichert became intimate with Georg Ebers' mother and favored his sister Paula.
Georg Ebers' oldest sister Martha became the wife of Lieutenant-Colonel Baron Curt von Brandenstein.
Georg Ebers' brother Martin was five years older than him.
Georg Ebers' sister Paula was three years older than him.
Georg Ebers' brother Ludwig (Ludo) was one and a half years older than him.
Ludo became a soldier and served as a Prussian officer in the war.
Georg Ebers and Ludo were frequently mistaken for twins.
Georg Ebers and Ludo shared everything and received gifts on each other's birthdays.
Georg Ebers did not learn to use 'I' and 'me' instead of 'we' and 'us' until comparatively late in life.
Georg Ebers' mother called him into her bed in the morning and told him fairy tales.
Georg Ebers' mother gave fairy tales the form of dramas in which he was permitted to be an actor.
Georg Ebers' favorite fairy tale was Little Red Riding Hood.
Georg Ebers played Snow-White while his mother played the wicked step-mother, the hunter, the dwarf, and the handsome prince.
Georg Ebers has written a volume of fairy tales himself.
Georg Ebers did not remember a time when he believed his own invented stories to be true.
Georg Ebers and his siblings could not always distinguish whether plausible tales belonged to fact or fiction.
Georg Ebers' mother's answer to questions about fact and fiction instantly set all doubts at rest.
Georg Ebers abhorred falsehood.
When seven or eight years old, Georg Ebers heard a boy tell a shameless lie and shrank in horror.
Georg Ebers and Ludo adhered to the truth more rigidly than other boys.
Georg Ebers' sister Paula was a fanatic in the cause of truth.
Georg Ebers' mother's hand scarcely ever touched his face except in a caress during his time in the Thiergarten.
Georg Ebers' mother moved to the Thiergarten with a heart oppressed by the deepest sorrow.
Georg Ebers' mother lived in the Thiergarten during part of his childhood.
Georg Ebers' mother rarely struck his face except in caresses.
Georg Ebers has bright and beautiful memories of his mother.
Georg Ebers' mother moved to the Thiergarten with a heart oppressed by deep sorrow.
Bernhardine Kron was the governess of the two eldest Ebers children.
Bernhardine Kron was a native of Mecklenburg.
Bernhardine Kron became a faithful friend to Georg Ebers' mother.
Georg Ebers' mother and Bernhardine Kron read together during quiet evenings.
Georg Ebers' mother read works of Racine and Corneille to Bernhardine Kron.
Georg Ebers' mother was a native of Holland.
Georg Ebers' mother had thorough mastery of French and English.
Georg Ebers' mother shared her intellectual possessions with her children.
Georg Ebers' father was deceased.
Georg Ebers' mother observed February 14th as the anniversary of his father's death.
Georg Ebers' mother withdrew from household members on the anniversary of his father's death.
Georg Ebers' father was buried in the Dreifaltigkeitskirchhof outside the Halle Gate.
Georg Ebers visited his father's grave with his mother in a carriage.
Georg Ebers gathered cornflowers, poppies, bluebells, daisies, ranunculus, and snapdragon to bring to graves.
Georg Ebers' mother's stone cross now stands beside his father's in the family burial place.
Moritz von Oppenfeld was the dearest of Georg Ebers' father's relatives.
Georg Ebers' mother used flowers and children to express love for the dead.
Georg Ebers' mother spoke of his father at the grave and offered forgiveness to punished children there.
Schleiermacher's grave lay near the Ebers family burial lot.
Schleiermacher died in 1834, three years before Georg Ebers was born.
Georg Ebers' mother had frequently met Schleiermacher.
Georg Ebers' mother knew Schleiermacher's enigmas.
Georg Ebers' mother favored external ceremonies despite her sincerity of feeling.
Georg Ebers and his siblings were freshly dressed on festal occasions.
Georg Ebers' mother gave silk scarves to birthday children.
Georg Ebers' mother's birthday was on July 25th.
Georg Ebers' mother held a picnic on her birthday.
Georg Ebers' mother attended church on Sunday.
Georg Ebers' mother insisted her daughters wear black on Good Friday.
Georg Ebers' family received Christmas gifts under a decorated tree.
Georg Ebers' mother gave gifts to poor people at Christmas.
Georg Ebers' oldest sister's nurse was the shoemaker Grossman's wife.
Georg Ebers' mother said 'No one ever grew poor by giving.'
Georg Ebers' mother used her social influence to help others advance.
A Christmas Man visited Georg Ebers' family disguised with a beard.
Knecht Ruprecht accompanied the Christmas Man in Georg Ebers' childhood.
Georg Ebers' family ate carp on Christmas Eve.
Georg Ebers has maintained Christmas customs from his childhood home.
Georg Ebers spent two Christmas Eves on the Nile.
Georg Ebers decorated a palm tree with candles when unable to procure a Christmas tree on the Nile.
Georg Ebers' mother never allowed children to be frightened by images of horror.
Georg Ebers' mother became angry when servants threatened children with the Black Man.
Georg Ebers' wife induced him to banish the Christmas Man and Knecht Ruprecht.
Georg Ebers' mother inspired love for the Christ Child and the Saviour.
Georg Ebers' mother saw Christ as the embodiment of love.
Georg Ebers came to the same conviction about Christ as his mother through investigation and thought.
Georg Ebers believes Christ widened the circle of love to include all mankind.
Georg Ebers' mother's life gave practical proof of human love.
Georg Ebers believes human love is the banner under which genuine progress of mankind has been made.
Georg Ebers' mother clasped his little hands in prayer before sleep.
Georg Ebers' mother gave him a good-night kiss before sleep.
Georg Ebers' mother came in full evening dress to hear him repeat his prayer before entertainments.
Georg Ebers' mother emphasized manners in greeting strangers and at table.
Georg Ebers' mother taught him fluent French at an early age.
Georg Ebers' mother employed bonnes who spoke French to the children.
Georg Ebers' mother reached Berlin with scanty knowledge of German.
Georg Ebers' mother's mastery of French secured pleasant things in Berlin.
Georg Ebers' mother valued French highly because of its charm and utility.
Georg Ebers has never-to-be-forgotten memories of travel due to his French ability.
Georg Ebers benefited greatly from his French ability during his period of study in Paris.
Georg Ebers admires Bismarck's perfect knowledge of French.
Georg Ebers noticed Bismarck uses French phrases to express ideas more briefly than German.
Georg Ebers' mother's concern with good manners did not deprive the children of natural freshness.
Georg Ebers and his brothers spent a large portion of boyhood amid surroundings that led them back to Nature.
Georg Ebers was not forbidden to play like genuine boys in Berlin.
Georg Ebers went to Keilhau as a boy of eleven.
Georg Ebers had two brothers.
Georg Ebers had sisters.
Georg Ebers was born in 1837.
Bismarck had perfect knowledge of French.
Bismarck used French phrases when mingling French with German to communicate more briefly.
Ebers' mother was solicitous concerning good manners and perfection in speaking French.
Ebers had three brothers.
Ebers spent a large portion of his boyhood amid surroundings that led back to Nature.
In Berlin, Ebers and his brothers were permitted to play like genuine boys.
Ebers had playmates of both sexes.
Ebers and his brothers built snow-men with help from their servant Kurschner.
In the house, Ebers and his family were obliged to behave courteously.
Gas was first introduced to Berlin by an English company about ten years before Ebers' birth.
The manufacturing city of Kottbus contained about ten thousand inhabitants during Ebers' school days.
In Ebers' childhood, gas was not used in the houses and theatres of Berlin.
Kerosene had not found its way to Germany during Ebers' childhood.
Rooms in Ebers' childhood were lighted by oil lamps and candles.
Servants burned tallow-dips.
Ebers studied by tallow-dips during his years at school in Keilhau.
Matches were not known in Ebers' early childhood.
Ebers remembers the tinder box in the kitchen with steel, flint, and threads dipped in sulphur.
Long, rough lucifer matches appeared after the tinder box era.
The gardener lit his pipe with flint, steel, and tinder.
The gun used had a firelock.
For writing, Ebers and his contemporaries used exclusively goose-quills.
Steel pens were invented soon after Ebers was born.
Steel pens had to combat violent prejudice in Ebers' first school.
At Ebers' first school, students were strictly forbidden to use steel pens.
The penknife played an important part on every writing-desk.
Ebers' mother's brothers included one resident of Java and one who lived as 'Opperhoofd' in Japan.
Letters from Ebers' mother's brothers took a long time to reach Berlin.
The Netherland embassy was used for sending letters or little gifts to Holland.
Express letters were the swiftest way of receiving or giving news in Ebers' youth.
There was a signal telegraph whose arms moved up and down in Government service.
When Ebers' mother was ill in Holland, a reply to an urgent telegram was received in Leipsic in eighteen minutes.
There was no railroad to Holland when Ebers was young.
Ebers was born in despotic Prussia.
Prussia was united to Austria and German states by a loosely formed league.
The Prussian nation first obtained liberties in 1848 which had been secured earlier by other German states.
Ebers was grateful to see the realization of the dream of German unification.
Ebers was a contemporary of Emperor William I, Bismarck, and Von Moltke.
Ebers witnessed the great deeds of William I, Bismarck, and Von Moltke as a man of mature years.
Ebers shared the enthusiasm evoked by William I, Bismarck, and Von Moltke.
Ebers looks back on the first part of his childhood as a beautiful, unshadowed dream.
Ebers has only disconnected images from his earliest childhood, not a series of events.
Ebers' mother lavished love and caresses on him as the youngest child.
The ringing of the little bell announcing the Christmas tree was the most blissful sound in Ebers' childhood.
Ebers' mother and grandmother told him stories about Snow-White, the Sleeping Beauty, giants, dwarfs, and Cinderella.
Ebers saw a picture of the three kings in purple robes kneeling before the Christ-Child.
Ebers never forgot the meaning of the picture of the three kings.
When Ebers was four years old, he went to his mother's home to attend his grandparents' golden wedding.
Ebers had an inborn love of travel.
The month spent on the journey to Holland seemed a period of very uncomfortable restlessness to Ebers.
A child realizes only a single detail of beauty, according to Ebers.
Ebers received a little green leather Bajazzo dressed in red and white from a relative.
Ebers received a box of candies from a friend of his mother for the trip.
Ebers' aunt Henriette was married to Adolphe Jones.
Adolphe Jones was a painter of animals.
Adolphe Jones shared a studio with the painter Verboeckhoven.
Adolphe Jones' flocks of sheep were very highly praised.
Ebers' aunt Henriette had jealousy that almost bordered on insanity.
Adolphe Jones was a jovial artist.
Adolphe Jones devoted himself earnestly to art.
Ebers' mother possessed the friendship of the animal painter Verboeckhoven through Adolphe Jones.
Ebers' mother possessed the friendship of Louis Gallait and his family.
Ebers passed many delightful hours in the society and home of Louis Gallait and his family.
Ebers' uncle was six feet tall.
Ebers' aunt Henriette possessed dazzling beauty in her youth.
The Duke of Wellington offered Ebers' aunt Henriette his arm at a famous ball in Brussels.
Ebers' mother remembered the Napoleonic days.
Ebers' mother saw Napoleon when he entered Rotterdam.
Ebers' mother saw Goethe.
Ebers' grandfather was a stately old gentleman.
Ebers' grandfather called him Georg Krullebol, meaning curly-head.
Ebers had a cousin called Georg von Gent.
On St. Nicholas day (December 5th), Ebers and his siblings found their shoes stuffed with gifts.
On Christmas Eve, a tree prepared for Ebers and his siblings attracted a crowd of curious spectators.
Ebers performed a little verse at his grandparents' golden wedding.
Ebers wore a short pink skirt, a wreath of roses, wings, a quiver, and carried a bow at the golden wedding.
Ebers' governess composed the little Cupid's speech.
Ebers' mother drilled him thoroughly in the speech.
Ebers felt no anxiety and embarrassment during his performance.
Ebers received loud applause at the golden wedding.
Ebers' grandfather wore short trousers called 'escarpins' and stockings reaching to the knee.
Ebers' grandmother was sixty-six years old at the golden wedding and had married before she was seventeen.
Ebers' grandmother wore a heavy white silk dress strewn with tiny bouquets at the golden wedding.
The dress was remodelled at the silver wedding.
The dress was left to Ebers' mother after his grandmother's death.
Ebers recollects his grandfather distinctly and his grandmother dimly.
A portrait of Ebers' grandmother was painted by Bradley, one of the best English portrait painters.
The portrait of Ebers' grandmother hung in his mother's room beside her husband's portrait.
The portrait is now one of Ebers' most cherished possessions.
Connoisseurs pronounce the portrait a masterpiece.
Black clouds gathered over the house during the golden wedding celebration.
Ebers' grandfather became ill during the golden wedding.
Ebers' mother's sunny face became anxious and sad during his grandfather's illness.
Ebers' mother rarely came to the children and her thoughts were far away while nursing her father.
Ebers' grandfather died.
Ebers' mother, pale and sorrowful, told the children that their grandfather was dead.
Children cannot understand the terrible solemnity of death, according to Ebers.
Women were weeping and men's eyes were reddened by tears after the grandfather's death.
Black clothes were being cut for the children in the nursery.
Ebers was pleased with his new mourning dress.
Ebers tried to make a suit for his Berlin Jack-in-the-box from scraps from the dressmaker's table.
The children were forbidden to laugh after the grandfather's death.
After a few days, Ebers' mother no longer checked the children's mirth.
Ebers' family stayed at Scheveningen.
The paths in the little garden at Scheveningen were strewn with shells.
Ebers and his siblings dug a big hole in the sand on the downs.
Ebers retained no remembrance of the sea during his stay at Scheveningen.
When Ebers beheld the sea in later years, it seemed as if he were greeting it for the first time.
The sea became dear and familiar to Ebers.
Ebers' grandmother passed away scarcely a year after his grandfather's death.
Ebers' grandmother died at the home of her son, a lawyer in The Hague.
Ebers' family went by steamer up the Rhine on the journey back.
Ebers' family stopped at Ehrenbreitstein to visit old Frau Mendelssohn.
Frau Mendelssohn was the mother of Ebers' guardian.
Frau Mendelssohn had an estate at Horchheim.
A carriage was sent for Ebers' family.
Spirited horses ran away during the carriage drive.
The horses would have dashed into the Rhine had not Ebers' brother Martin saved them.
Ebers' brother Martin was eleven years old at the time.
Martin was sitting on the box by the coachman.
Ebers had seen many salmon in the kitchen.
Ebers resolved to fish for a salmon from the steamer.
Ebers tied a bit of candy to a string and dropped it from the deck.
The fish disdained the sweet bait.
Ebers' early awakened love of sport kept him patiently fishing.
Ebers' mother went down into the cabin to rest.
There was a loud uproar on the ship.
Georg Ebers had a brother named Martin who was eleven years old at the time of a carriage incident.
Martin saved the family from being dashed into the Rhine by runaway horses while sitting on the box by the coachman.
Georg Ebers attempted to fish for salmon from a steamer using candy tied to a string.
Georg Ebers had an early awakened love of sport.
A crazy Englishman being transported to an insane asylum jumped into the river while Georg's mother was resting in the cabin.
Georg's mother rushed from the cabin in maternal anxiety when she heard cries of someone falling into the river.
The Englishman was rescued unharmed from the river.
Georg Ebers took many trips as a young child, usually to Dresden where his grandmother Ebers lived.
Friedrich Froebel, the founder of the kindergarten system, told Georg that he would wander far through the world based on his wide-set teeth.
Georg Ebers lived on Lennestrasse in Berlin during his childhood.
Lenne was the park superintendent and a friend of Georg's mother.
Lenne was a landscape gardener who transformed the gardens of Sans-Souci and the Pfaueninsel at Potsdam.
Lenne laid out the magnificent park on Babelsberg for Emperor William I when he was Prince of Prussia.
Lenne designed the Zoological Garden in Berlin.
Lenne enlarged the Thiergarten to serve as a 'lung' for the people.
King Frederick William IV often summoned Lenne to dine with him at Potsdam.
Georg Ebers and his brother called Lenne 'Uncle Lenne' and walked with him hand in hand.
The area near Potsdam Gate was called the 'Geheimerath-Quarter.'
Georg Ebers's oldest brother and Paul Seiffart took a firework to school where it went off.
Georg's mother sent her oldest son to Keilhau educational institution after the firework incident.
Adolph Diesterweg recommended Keilhau to Georg's mother.
Georg Ebers's conduct was regulated according to his mental and outward connection with his mother.
A storm and stress period in Georg's youth threatened to loosen his close connection with his mother.
Georg's mother woke him, prepared him for school, took him on walks, and read to him when lamps were lighted.
Georg Ebers sketched while his sisters sewed during evening time with his mother.
Georg's brother Ludo sometimes crocheted during evening time with his mother.
Georg Ebers listened to Robinson Crusoe, the Arabian Nights, Gulliver's Travels, and Don Quixote as a child.
Georg Ebers heard Grimm's fairy tales read aloud by his mother.
Frau Amalie Beer and Moritz von Oppenfeld subscribed for opera boxes and sent Georg's family the key when they did not use them.
Georg Ebers heard most of the operas produced at that time as a boy.
Georg Ebers saw ballets that Frederick William IV was especially fond of.
Taglioni arranged ballets admirably.
Georg Ebers found the comic play 'Robert and Bertram' by Ludwig Schneider more delightful than grand operas.
Don Giovanni's scene with the statue and the conspiracy in the Huguenots stirred Georg deeply when he was nine or ten years old.
Georg Ebers possessed barely the average amount of musical talent.
Orpheus's cry 'Eurydice!' rang in Georg's ears for a long time after hearing it as a child.
Georg Ebers attended theatrical performances too young, which he admits may have been harmful.
Georg Ebers later succeeded in describing large bodies of men seized by strong excitement in his novels.
Georg Ebers's novels had dramatic movement, vivid scenes, and splendour.
Georg Ebers had early intercourse with artists and opportunity to see their work.
The most numerous and best impulses for artistic development come from childhood.
Large cities produce active, practical men and great scholars, but few artists and poets.
Georg Ebers's mother removed him from Berlin life in time.
Georg Ebers was never really taught to read formally.
Georg's brother Ludo was a year and a half older than Georg.
Georg Ebers learned to read by picking up Speckter's Fables and reading a few words while playing.
Georg Ebers and Ludo were taught together after Georg demonstrated he could read.
A governess initially had charge of Georg and Ludo's education.
Georg and Ludo attended a little school kept by Herr Liebe in Schulgarten Strasse.
Herr Liebe's school was attended almost entirely by children from their circle of acquaintances.
Herr Liebe let his students do more digging in his garden and playing or singing than actual study.
Herr Liebe had an only child, a pretty little girl named Clara.
Georg Ebers learned to write from Herr Liebe.
Herr Liebe took his students on long walks in summer, frequently to the country seat of Herr Korte.
Georg Ebers brought his mother great bunches of flowers from excursions.
Candidate Woltmann was an excellent story-teller and tutor for a family in their circle of friends.
Georg Ebers early felt a desire to share with those he loved whatever charmed him.
Georg Ebers first heard the names of Greek heroes from Candidate Woltmann.
Georg Ebers begged his mother for Schwab's Tales of Classic Antiquity after hearing about Greek heroes.
Georg and Ludo received Schwab's Tales of Classic Antiquity on Ludo's birthday in September.
The story of the Trojan War made a deeper impression on Georg than the Arabian Nights.
Georg Ebers and Ludo played the Trojan War and aspired to act Hector, Achilles, or Ajax.
Herr Liebe treated his students to cake and wine on his daughter Clara's birthday.
Herr Liebe composed a festal song with a changing refrain for Clara's birthday celebrations.
Karl von Holtei had to fit the name Ferdinand into 'Gotterhalte Franz den Kaiser' after Emperor Francis's death.
Herr Liebe was a churchwarden and had the honour of taking up the collection.
Georg Ebers and his schoolmates assisted Herr Liebe in sorting collection money.
Counters and buttons from clothing were frequently found mixed in with the collection money.
Georg Ebers and his schoolmates often passed through the Potsdam Gate instead of going directly home from school.
Georg Ebers and his schoolmates fought with pupils from a humbler grade of school.
The pupils from the humbler school called Georg's group 'Privy Councillor's youngsters.'
Georg's group called the other pupils 'Knoten.'
The 'Knoten' were led by boys whose father kept a flower cellar at the head of Leipzigerstrasse.
Georg Ebers and his schoolmates used school knapsacks as weapons in their fights.
When Georg Ebers got into a passion he was as wild as a fighting cock.
Ludo could deal hard blows despite being quiet.
Georg Ebers and his friends called themselves the 'Knoten'.
The rival group was called the 'Geheimrathsjoren'.
The rival group was led by boys whose father kept a flower cellar at the head of Leipzigerstrasse.
Georg Ebers and his friends fought with the rival group in courtyards using school knapsacks as weapons.
Ludo was quiet but could deal hard blows.
The fights between the groups were often interrupted by janitors or house inhabitants.
A fat woman, probably a cook, seized Georg Ebers by the collar and pushed him into the street during a fight.
The Berlin revolution led to the end of the battles with the 'Knoten'.
Georg Ebers' mother sent him and his siblings away to Keilhau.
Georg Ebers' sisters attended a school kept by Fraulein Sollmann in the Dorotheenstrasse.
Georg Ebers had a tutor.
Georg Ebers attended the Schmidt school in the Leipziger Platz beginning at age ten.
Brother Martin attended Keilhau.
Georg Ebers had tin soldiers and a brass cannon as toys.
Georg Ebers and his siblings could build castles and cathedrals with blocks.
Georg Ebers' sister Martha was the eldest and was sweet and kind.
Emma Baeyer was the daughter of General Baeyer, who conducted the measuring of the meridian for central Europe.
Anna Bisting was pretty and lively.
Gretchen Bugler was a handsome, merry girl who married Paul Heyse and died young.
Clara and Agnes Mitscherlich were daughters of a celebrated chemist.
Agnes Mitscherlich was especially dear to Georg Ebers' childish heart.
Gustel Grimm was the daughter of Wilhelm Grimm.
Max Geppert was Georg Ebers' playmate.
Georg Ebers was in love with Annchen, the sister of Max Geppert.
Annchen was the most intimate friend of Georg Ebers' sister Paula.
Georg Ebers hid Annchen's overshoes during a dancing lesson on a rainy Saturday evening.
Georg Ebers celebrated his silver wedding two years before writing this autobiography.
Georg Ebers' wife resembled Annchen.
Georg Ebers values women whose womanliness and gentle charm touch the heart before one thinks of intellect or beauty.
Georg Ebers' mother smiled at his childhood romantic affairs.
Georg Ebers' sister Paula showed a preference for Paul, the son of Privy-Councillor Seiffart.
Paul was born on the same November day as Paula, though several years earlier.
Paul was the friend, comrade, and contemporary of Martin.
Georg Ebers' mother and the Seiffarts' mother were bosom friends.
Puppet shows were conducted at the Seiffarts' residence on a pretty little stage in the great hall.
Paula was from childhood a singular young creature who did not enjoy all the amusements of her age.
Martha's eyes sparkled joyously when there was a dance in prospect.
Paula was tall and slender and looked extremely pretty in a ball dress.
Paula was gay, active, bold as a boy, clever, and full of droll fancies.
Paula dwelt much in her own thoughts.
Paula's journals came to Georg Ebers after their mother's death.
Paula confessed at thirteen that she liked no worldly pleasures.
Paula was a very truthful child.

The Silesia position combined rest with occupation suited to Langethal's tastes.
Langethal was to share the labour of teaching in Silesia with another instructor.
The other instructor was to take charge of the exact sciences, with which Langethal was less familiar.
Langethal was permitted to teach his brother with the young Counts Stolberg.
Langethal accepted the position in Silesia.
